content_en:
The additional coverage that Lisan al-Mizan provides beyond the Mizan is one of its most distinctive contributions. Ibn Hajar extended the Mizan's coverage to include narrators found in works of Sufi biography — a domain that adh-Dhahabi had not addressed systematically — providing evaluations of transmitters associated with the Sufi tradition and helping to situate the hadith content of Sufi literature within the framework of the classical authentication methodology.

The coverage of narrators in Shia and Mutazili sources is another area where Lisan al-Mizan extends the Mizan. Adh-Dhahabi had addressed some figures from these communities but had not attempted comprehensive coverage. Ibn Hajar's additions in this area reflect his broader knowledge of the non-Sunni hadith and biographical literature and his conviction that a comprehensive narrator dictionary should address all the relevant sources.

Ibn Hajar's corrections of adh-Dhahabi are instructive case studies in the methodology of narrator criticism. In several cases, he argues that adh-Dhahabi's assessment was too harsh — that a narrator whom adh-Dhahabi classified as weak was actually reliable enough to be acceptable as a corroborating witness or even as a primary source. In other cases, he argues that adh-Dhahabi was too lenient, pointing to evidence that a narrator was less reliable than adh-Dhahabi's assessment suggested. These debates are not merely academic: they have practical implications for how specific hadith are evaluated.

The biographical entries in Lisan al-Mizan also provide historical information about the social and intellectual environments in which different narrators lived and transmitted. Ibn Hajar draws on a wide range of sources — local histories, biographical dictionaries specific to cities or regions, Sufi biographical works — to provide contextual information that goes beyond the narrow focus on transmission reliability. This broader biographical dimension makes the work valuable for social historians as well as for hadith specialists. Ibn Hajar's method of drawing on regional biographical dictionaries — works specific to individual cities or scholarly communities — preserved information that would otherwise be inaccessible to researchers who lack the resources to consult these specialized local sources. In this way, Lisan al-Mizan functions not only as a narrator-criticism reference but as a window into the geographic and social diversity of hadith transmission across the Islamic world.
